---
title: "Method"
---

## Dataset

Extending the model with segmentation instances instances requires an accurately segmented and tokenized dataset aligned with the already present modalities. For this purpose, we extend the multiCLEVR dataset from EPFL's COM-304 course, which is composed of 50'000 train and 5'000 validation images, with 10 augmentation each.

## Labeling & Tokenization

The RGB dataset is pseudolabeled with [SAM v2.1 Small](https://github.com/facebookresearch/sam2), filtered for stable masks, and normalized to `64x64` size using ROI Align to better conserve the complex edges. Each mask is then processed by the pre-trained [4M SAM instance tokenizer (1k - 64x64)](https://huggingface.co/EPFL-VILAB/4M_tokenizers_sam-instance_1k_64), resulting in 16 (`4x4`, flattened) tokens per mask.

The resulting representation for each instance segmentation is 22 tokens: 2 coordinates tokens, 4 bounding boxes  tokens in XYXY format, and 16 tokens for the mask.

An offset is added to each of the coordinates (6 tokens total) to ensure that the logit computations does not conflict with each other, thus resulting in clearer differentiation between the different data type. Furthermore, the tokens are padded with the  `2561` token to reach a `256` length.

The final codebook is thus `2562`, and each mask is as follow:

| | X COORD | Y COORD | X MIN | Y MIN | X MAX | Y MAX | INSTANCE MASK (16 TOKENS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Codebook | 1024-1280 | 1280-1536 | 1536-1792 | 2048-2304 | 2304-2560 | 0-1024 |

![](images/tokenizer_pipeline.png){fig-alt="Tokenization pipeline for train sample 20" width="100%"}

Figure: Tokenization pipeline for train sample 20

### Mask quality

We observe that the reconstructed mask tend to have unprecise edges and larger uncovered areas cause by the loss of information during the downsizing to a smaller dimensionality space during tokenization. This is particularly accentuated for larger masks. Furthermore, some artifacts from from shadows or faces of the polygons are tokenized, leading to more noisy data.

![](images/labeling.png){fig-alt="Examples of tokenized masks" width="100%"}

Figure: Examples of tokenized masks. First row: original cropped RGB image. Second row: SAMv2.1 Small AMG masked image. Third row: Our tokenized & detokenized mask

Observing the coverage ratio ($\text{Coverage Ratio} = \frac{\text{Area of Union of All Masks}}{\text{Total Image Area}}$, low is worse) and the redundancy index ($\text{Redundancy index} = \frac{\sum{\text{Individual Mask Areas}}}{\text{Area of Union of All Masks}}$, high is worse), we obtain decent results, showing that the instance masks generally cover most of the image and there is relatively little overlap between them.

| Type | Split | AVG COVERAGE RATE | CR < 0.92 (%) | AVG REDUNDANCY INDEX | RI > 1.05 (%) |
| Tokenized Mask | Train | $0.964 \pm 0.056$ | $2.166$ | $1.022 \pm 0.016$ | $3.834$ |
| Tokenized Mask | Validation | $0.965 \pm 0.051$ | $1.980$ | $1.021 \pm 0.015$ |  $3.260$ |
| AMG Mask | Train (5'000 subset) | $0.983 \pm 0.009$ | $0.240$ | $1.003 \pm 0.011$ | $1.520$ |

Our 256 array length limit causes certain masks to be cropped out if an image has more or equal 11 instances (244 tokens). In the violin plot below, we observe how around 1.5-2% exceed this limits, losing at least a mask.

![](images/mask_ratio.png){fig-alt="Mask_ratio" width="100%"}

Figure: Number of instance masks per image




## Training setup

The reported 5-modality model is trained jointly from scratch. All encoder and decoder parameters are initialized together, and SAM is treated as an additional masked prediction target within the same multimodal framework.

## Dataset structure

- 50,000 training scenes.
- 5,000 validation scenes.
- 10 augmentations per scene.
- One aligned file per modality per sample.




1. A figure of the labeling/tokenization pipeline.
2. A compact diagram of the architecture.
3. A short explanation of padding, truncation, and sequence length choices.
